nl_task_title: Uploading Documents with Secure Storage 0/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Uploading Documents with Secure Storage
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, Database

Interactions:

User Action: The user uploads a document (e.g., an ID or contract) through the mobile app.
Mobile App: Sends the document along with the session token to the BFF.
BFF Validation:
Validates the session token with Azure AD.
Checks user permissions to ensure they are authorized to upload documents.
Storage Process:
The BFF saves metadata about the document (e.g., file name, size, upload timestamp) in the database.
The actual document is securely stored in cloud storage (e.g., Azure Blob Storage).
Response:
On successful upload, the BFF returns a confirmation to the app.
If the user is unauthorized or the file exceeds size limits, an appropriate error is returned.
input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

Purpose: Uploading Documents with Secure Storage
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, Database

Interactions:

User Action: The user uploads a document (e.g., an ID or contract) through the mobile app.
Mobile App: Sends the document along with the session token to the BFF.
BFF Validation:
Validates the session token with Azure AD.
Checks user permissions to ensure they are authorized to upload documents.
Storage Process:
The BFF saves metadata about the document (e.g., file name, size, upload timestamp) in the database.
The actual document is securely stored in cloud storage (e.g., Azure Blob Storage).
Response:
On successful upload, the BFF returns a confirmation to the app.
If the user is unauthorized or the file exceeds size limits, an appropriate error is returned.
expected_output:
sequenceDiagram
participant U as User
participant MA as Mobile App
participant BFF as Backend for Frontend
participant AAD as Azure AD
participant DB as Database
participant AS as Azure Blob Storage

Note over U: Upload Document
U->>MA: Upload document
MA->>BFF: Send document (with session token)
activate BFF
BFF->>AAD: Validate session token
deactivate BFF
activate AAD
AAD-->>BFF: Token validated
deactivate AAD
activate BFF
BFF->>AAD: Check user permissions
deactivate BFF
activate AAD
AAD-->>BFF: Permissions valid
deactivate AAD
activate BFF

alt Invalid token, insufficient permissions, or file too large
  BFF-->>MA: Error message
else Upload successful
  BFF->>DB: Save document metadata
  activate DB
  DB-->>BFF: Metadata saved
  deactivate DB

  BFF->>AS: Store document
  activate AS
  AS-->>BFF: Document stored
  deactivate AS

  BFF-->>MA: Confirmation
  
end
deactivate BFF
llm_response:
```sequenceDiagram
participant User
participant MobileApp as Mobile App
participant BFF
participant AzureAD as Azure AD
participant DB as Database
participant CloudStorage as Azure Blob Storage

User->>MobileApp: Upload Document
MobileApp->>BFF: POST /documents (file, session_token)
activate BFF

Note over BFF: Validate session and permissions
BFF->>AzureAD: GET /validate_token (session_token)
activate AzureAD
AzureAD-->>BFF: Token Validation Result
deactivate AzureAD

alt Token Invalid or Unauthorized
    BFF-->>MobileApp: 401 Unauthorized
else Token Valid
    Note over BFF: Check file size limits
    alt File Size Exceeds Limit
        BFF-->>MobileApp: 413 Payload Too Large
    else File Valid
        BFF->>DB: POST /metadata (filename, size, timestamp)
        activate DB
        DB-->>BFF: 201 Created (Metadata Saved)
        deactivate DB
        
        BFF->>CloudStorage: PUT /blob (file_data)
        activate CloudStorage
        CloudStorage-->>BFF: 200 OK (File Stored)
        deactivate CloudStorage
        
        BFF-->>MobileApp: 200 OK (Upload Successful)
    end
end

deactivate BFF
MobileApp-->>User: Display Upload Status
```
llm_judge_syntax_response: 1.000; Proper MermaidJS syntax. All participants are declared with valid `participant` syntax (including `as` aliases). Activations and deactivations are correctly paired and balanced for BFF, AzureAD, DB, and CloudStorage. The `alt`/`else`/`end` blocks are properly structured and nested (an inner `alt` for file size within the outer token validation `alt`), with all branches closed by corresponding `end` statements.
llm_judge_mermaid_only_response: 0.950; The response is fully wrapped in a Markdown code block labeled with ```sequenceDiagram and contains only Mermaid sequence diagram syntax with no extra prose. The structure (participants, messages, alt blocks, notes) is valid and should be renderable by Mermaid. Minor concern: some Mermaid renderers expect the first line inside the block to be just `sequenceDiagram` rather than including it in the fence, but most will still parse this correctly.
llm_judge_logic_response: 0.980; Syntax is valid MermaidJS, with correctly declared participants, proper use of `alt/else/end`, and matching activations/deactivations (BFF is activated once and deactivated after all branches). Every request has a corresponding response: MobileApp↔BFF, BFF↔AzureAD, BFF↔DB, BFF↔CloudStorage, and MobileApp↔User are all closed with replies. Alternate flows are clearly represented: invalid/unauthorized token branch, file-too-large branch, and successful upload branch, with nested `alt` for file size inside the valid-token path. The diagram covers all described interactions: upload from user, token validation, permission implied in token validation note, metadata save to DB, secure storage in blob, and success/error responses. The only minor deviation from the reference is that permission checking is not modeled as a separate explicit call to Azure AD but is conceptually included in the validation step; this is logically equivalent and complete, so only a very small deduction.
llm_judge_completeness_response: 0.980; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and participants are correctly defined and ordered (User → Mobile App → BFF → Azure AD → DB → Cloud Storage). All key interactions from the prompt are present: user uploads via app, app sends file and token to BFF, BFF validates token with Azure AD, checks authorization (implicitly combined with token validity in the “Token Invalid or Unauthorized” branch), stores metadata in DB, stores file in cloud storage, and returns confirmation. Every request has a corresponding response, and activations/deactivations are used correctly. Alternate flows are handled with nested `alt` blocks for invalid/unauthorized token and oversized file, both returning appropriate error responses. The only minor gap is that permission checking is not modeled as a distinct call to Azure AD but is folded into the token validation result; behaviorally it still covers the described decision point. Overall coverage and error handling are complete with only that small abstraction difference.
llm_judge_activation_handling_response: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS, and activations are generally used correctly: BFF, AzureAD, DB, and CloudStorage are each activated when they receive a message and deactivated after sending their response. Every `activate` has a matching `deactivate`, and there are no `deactivate` calls without a prior `activate`. The single long activation of BFF spanning the entire validation and storage flow is consistent and closed after all branches, which keeps control clear, though a slightly more granular activation/deactivation around each external call (as in the reference) could further improve traceability. Overall, the use of activation/deactivation is correct and aids readability.
llm_judge_error_and_status_tracking_resp: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and uses participants, activation, deactivation, alt/else, and notes correctly. Status updates are explicit: HTTP codes and messages are returned for unauthorized (401), file too large (413), and successful upload (200 OK, plus “Display Upload Status” to the user). Success and failure flows are clearly separated via nested `alt Token Invalid or Unauthorized` and `alt File Size Exceeds Limit` blocks versus the normal path that saves metadata, stores the blob, and returns success. Error handling is explicit and specific for each major failure condition (invalid/unauthorized token, size limit violation), with appropriate early returns. The diagram tracks the state of key entities reasonably well through activations and responses (BFF, Azure AD, DB, CloudStorage, MobileApp, User), though it doesn’t model internal state flags, just the interaction flow; this is the only minor limitation.

Purpose: Uploading Documents with Secure Storage
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, Database

Interactions:

User Action: The user uploads a document (e.g., an ID or contract) through the mobile app.
Mobile App: Sends the document along with the session token to the BFF.
BFF Validation:
Validates the session token with Azure AD.
Checks user permissions to ensure they are authorized to upload documents.
Storage Process:
The BFF saves metadata about the document (e.g., file name, size, upload timestamp) in the database.
The actual document is securely stored in cloud storage (e.g., Azure Blob Storage).
Response:
On successful upload, the BFF returns a confirmation to the app.
If the user is unauthorized or the file exceeds size limits, an appropriate error is returned.
expected_output:
sequenceDiagram
participant User
participant MobileApp
participant BackendForFrontend
participant AzureAD
participant Database
participant AzureBlobStorage

Note over User: Upload Document
User->>MobileApp: Upload document
MobileApp->>BackendForFrontend: Send document (with session token)
activate BackendForFrontend
BackendForFrontend->>AzureAD: Validate session token
deactivate BackendForFrontend
activate AzureAD
AzureAD-->>BackendForFrontend: Token validated
deactivate AzureAD
activate BackendForFrontend
BackendForFrontend->>AzureAD: Check user permissions
deactivate BackendForFrontend
activate AzureAD
AzureAD-->>BackendForFrontend: Permissions valid
deactivate AzureAD
activate BackendForFrontend

alt Invalid token, insufficient permissions, or file too large
  BackendForFrontend-->>MobileApp: Error message
else Upload successful
  BackendForFrontend->>Database: Save document metadata
  activate Database
  Database-->>BackendForFrontend: Metadata saved
  deactivate Database

  BackendForFrontend->>AzureBlobStorage: Store document
  activate AzureBlobStorage
  AzureBlobStorage-->>BackendForFrontend: Document stored
  deactivate AzureBlobStorage

  BackendForFrontend-->>MobileApp: Confirmation
  
end
deactivate BackendForFrontend
llm_response:
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant MobileApp as Mobile App
    participant BFF
    participant AzureAD as Azure AD
    participant DB as Database
    participant CloudStorage as Cloud Storage

    User->>MobileApp: Uploads Document
    MobileApp->>BFF: POST /documents (file, session_token)
    activate BFF

    Note over BFF: Validate Session & Permissions
    BFF->>AzureAD: GET /validate-token (session_token)
    activate AzureAD
    AzureAD-->>BFF: Token Validation Result
    deactivate AzureAD

    alt Token Invalid or Unauthorized
        BFF-->>MobileApp: 401 Unauthorized / 403 Forbidden
        deactivate BFF
    else Token Valid
        alt File Size Exceeds Limit
            BFF-->>MobileApp: 413 Payload Too Large
            deactivate BFF
        else File Valid
            Note over BFF: Secure Storage Process
            BFF->>CloudStorage: PUT /blob (file_data)
            activate CloudStorage
            CloudStorage-->>BFF: 200 OK (file_url)
            deactivate CloudStorage

            BFF->>DB: POST /document-metadata (filename, size, timestamp, file_url)
            activate DB
            DB-->>BFF: 201 Created
            deactivate DB

            BFF-->>MobileApp: 200 OK (Upload Confirmation)
            deactivate BFF
        end
    end
```

Overall, it is syntactically and structurally correct MermaidJS.

Every request has a corresponding response (including Azure AD, Cloud Storage, and DB). Alternate flows are clearly modeled with nested `alt` blocks: invalid/unauthorized token, file too large, and successful upload. The diagram covers all described interactions: upload from user, token validation, permission check (implicitly combined with token validation in the first alt), metadata save, secure storage, and success/error responses. The only minor deviation from the reference is that token invalid and unauthorized are grouped together and permission checking is not shown as a separate explicit call to Azure AD, but this is logically equivalent and still complete.
llm_judge_completeness_response: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and participants are correctly ordered and complete (User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, DB, Cloud Storage). All key interactions are present: user upload, app sending document and token, BFF validating token with Azure AD, checking authorization (implicitly combined in “Token Invalid or Unauthorized”), storing file in cloud storage, saving metadata in DB, and returning confirmation. Minor deviation: the prompt separates “validates session token” and “checks user permissions” as two distinct steps, while the diagram merges them into a single validation result, and it doesn’t explicitly show a separate permission-check call. This is a small loss of granularity but not a major behavioral gap. Overall coverage and error handling are very good.
llm_judge_activation_handling_response: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS. Activations are generally used correctly: BFF, AzureAD, CloudStorage, and DB are all activated when they receive a message and deactivated after sending their response. Every `activate` has a matching `deactivate`, and there are no `deactivate` calls without a prior `activate`. The nesting through the `alt` blocks is consistent, and BFF is deactivated in each terminal branch, which helps trace control flow. A minor readability improvement could be to activate MobileApp when it receives the initial user request or to avoid deactivating BFF multiple times in different branches by deactivating once after the `alt` block, but these are stylistic rather than correctness issues. Overall, the activations/deactivations significantly aid readability and traceability.
llm_judge_error_and_status_tracking_resp: 0.950; Syntax is valid MermaidJS and uses participants, messages, alt/else, notes, and activation/deactivation correctly. Status updates are explicit via HTTP status codes and descriptive responses (401/403, 413, 200/201) for each major step. Success and failure flows are clearly separated with nested `alt` blocks: first for token validity/authorization, then for file size vs valid file, with a distinct success path including storage and metadata save. Error-handling is effective and specific, covering invalid/unauthorized token and oversized file, each returning appropriate errors to the Mobile App. The diagram tracks key entity states implicitly through the sequence of validations, storage, and DB writes, and uses notes to clarify processing stages, though it does not model internal state variables explicitly. Overall, it is very strong on error cases and status updates with only minor room for more granular state tracking.

nl_task_title: Real-Time Notifications for Workflow Updates 1/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Real-Time Notifications for Workflow Updates
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, Database

Interactions:
User Action: A technician updates a workflow task (e.g., marking a job as completed) from their mobile app.
Mobile App: Sends the task update to the BFF with the session token.
BFF Validation:
Validates the session token with Azure AD.
Checks the user’s role and permissions for the specific workflow task.
Database Update:
The BFF updates the workflow task status in the database.
It triggers an event to notify stakeholders (e.g., a dispatcher or customer).
Real-Time Notification:
The BFF uses a notification service (e.g., Azure Notification Hubs or Firebase) to send a real-time push notification to the mobile app of all relevant stakeholders.
Notifications may include details like the updated status, timestamp, and who updated the task.
Response:
The app displays the updated workflow details and confirms the task status change.
If the update fails (e.g., invalid permissions), an error message is sent to the app.

nl_task_title: Real-Time Notifications for Workflow Updates 1/III
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Real-Time Notifications for Workflow Updates
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Azure AD, Database

Interactions:
User Action: A technician updates a workflow task (e.g., marking a job as completed) from their mobile app.
Mobile App: Sends the task update to the BFF with the session token.
BFF Validation:
Validates the session token with Azure AD.
Checks the user’s role and permissions for the specific workflow task.
Database Update:
The BFF updates the workflow task status in the database.
It triggers an event to notify stakeholders (e.g., a dispatcher or customer).
Real-Time Notification:
The BFF uses a notification service (e.g., Azure Notification Hubs or Firebase) to send a real-time push notification to the mobile app of all relevant stakeholders.
Notifications may include details like the updated status, timestamp, and who updated the task.
Response:
The app displays the updated workflow details and confirms the task status change.
If the update fails (e.g., invalid permissions), an error message is sent to the app.

nl_task_title: OAuth login flow for a mobile application 2/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: OAuth login flow for a mobile application
Main components: User, Mobile app, BFF, Azure AD, Database
Interactions:
User enters the credentials from the mobile application.
Mobile application redirects the user to Azure AD's authentication page.
User enters credentials on Azure AD's login page.
Azure AD validates the credentials.
If credentials are valid, Azure AD returns an authorization code to the mobile application (or BFF).
If credentials are incorrect, Azure AD returns an error response (invalid_grant), and the mobile app displays an error message.
If the account is locked due to multiple failed attempts, Azure AD returns an account_locked error, prompting the user to reset their password.
Mobile application sends the authorization code to BFF.
BFF exchanges the authorization code with Azure AD for an access token (and optionally a refresh token).
If the authorization code is expired or invalid, Azure AD returns invalid_grant, and the mobile app must restart the login process.
BFF stores the refresh token (if applicable) in the database.
BFF sends the access token back to the mobile application.
Mobile application uses the access token to access protected APIs.
If the access token is expired or revoked, the API returns invalid_token, and the mobile app must refresh it.
If the token lacks required scopes, the API returns insufficient_scope, and the mobile app prompts the user for additional permissions.
Mobile application requests a new access token using the refresh token when needed.
If the refresh token is expired or revoked, Azure AD returns refresh_token_expired, forcing a re-login.

nl_task_title: OAuth login flow for a mobile application 2/II
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: OAuth login flow for a mobile application
Main components: User, Mobile app, BFF, Azure AD, Database
Interactions:
User enters the credentials from the mobile application.
Mobile application redirects the user to Azure AD's authentication page.
User enters credentials on Azure AD's login page.
Azure AD validates the credentials.
If credentials are valid, Azure AD returns an authorization code to the mobile application (or BFF).
If credentials are incorrect, Azure AD returns an error response (invalid_grant), and the mobile app displays an error message.
If the account is locked due to multiple failed attempts, Azure AD returns an account_locked error, prompting the user to reset their password.
Mobile application sends the authorization code to BFF.
BFF exchanges the authorization code with Azure AD for an access token (and optionally a refresh token).
If the authorization code is expired or invalid, Azure AD returns invalid_grant, and the mobile app must restart the login process.
BFF stores the refresh token (if applicable) in the database.
BFF sends the access token back to the mobile application.
Mobile application uses the access token to access protected APIs.
If the access token is expired or revoked, the API returns invalid_token, and the mobile app must refresh it.
If the token lacks required scopes, the API returns insufficient_scope, and the mobile app prompts the user for additional permissions.
Mobile application requests a new access token using the refresh token when needed.
If the refresh token is expired or revoked, Azure AD returns refresh_token_expired, forcing a re-login.

nl_task_title: Send a notification to an end user via the Notification Server 3/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Send a notification to an end user via the Notification Server.
Main Components: End User, Mobile App, BFF (Backend for Frontend), Notification Server, APNS/FCM, SOR (System of Record)
Interactions:
1. Event Trigger: A specific event occurs (e.g., order update, message received) in SOR that requires notifying the end user.
2. BFF Notification Request: The BFF sends a request to the Notification Server, including details such as the target user/device, notification type, and message content.
3. Notification Delivery: The Notification Server pushes the notification to the respective mobile app using push notification service (e.g., APNs for iOS, FCM for Android).
4. Mobile App Handling: The Mobile App receives the notification, processes it (e.g., displaying an alert, updating UI)
5. User Interaction: The end user interacts with the notification (e.g., taps to open the app, dismisses it, or takes an action).

nl_task_title: Send a notification to an end user via the Notification Server 3/II
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Send a notification to an end user via the Notification Server.
Main Components: End User, Mobile App, BFF (Backend for Frontend), Notification Server, APNS/FCM, SOR (System of Record)
Interactions:
1. Event Trigger: A specific event occurs (e.g., order update, message received) in SOR that requires notifying the end user.
2. BFF Notification Request: The BFF sends a request to the Notification Server, including details such as the target user/device, notification type, and message content.
3. Notification Delivery: The Notification Server pushes the notification to the respective mobile app using push notification service (e.g., APNs for iOS, FCM for Android).
4. Mobile App Handling: The Mobile App receives the notification, processes it (e.g., displaying an alert, updating UI)
5. User Interaction: The end user interacts with the notification (e.g., taps to open the app, dismisses it, or takes an action).
input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

nl_task_title: Read water meter 4/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Read water meter
Main components: User, Mobile app, BFF, MDM, Database
Interactions:
- User creates login to the app
- Scans the meter and send Meter ID to MDM through BFF
- if Meter available 
  - MDM responds Water Address, Property Type, Past Meter reads
  - User validates the data
  - if address is not valid
    - if address available in map
      - user selects address from Map
    - else
      - user collects data required to create Unique Address ID
  - User colelcts the meter read
  - User submits the form. Formdata is sent to BFF for further processing.
- else
  - MDM responds as No Meter

participant DB as Database

U->>MA: Login
MA->>BFF: Authenticate
activate BFF
BFF->>DB: Authenticate
activate DB
DB-->>BFF: Authenticated
deactivate DB
BFF-->>MA: Authenticated
deactivate BFF

U->>MA: Scan Meter
MA->>BFF: Send Meter ID
activate BFF
BFF->>DB: Log meter scan activity
BFF->>MDM: Get Meter Info (Meter ID)
alt Meter available
  MDM-->>BFF: Water Address, Property Type, Past Meter reads

  deactivate BFF

    else

    end
  end

  U->>MA: Submit Form
  MA->>BFF: FormData
  activate BFF
  BFF->>DB: Log User activity
  BFF->>MDM: Process FormData
  deactivate BFF
else Meter unavailable
  MDM-->>BFF: No Meter

llm_response:
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Mobile as Mobile App
    participant BFF
    participant MDM
    participant DB

    Note over User, Mobile: User Login
    User->>Mobile: Enter Credentials
    Mobile->>BFF: POST /login
    activate BFF
    BFF->>DB: Validate User
    activate DB
    DB-->>BFF: User Valid
    deactivate DB
    BFF-->>Mobile: Auth Token
    deactivate BFF
    Mobile-->>User: Login Success

    Note over User, MDM: Read Water Meter Flow
    User->>Mobile: Scan Meter ID
    Mobile->>BFF: GET /meter/{id}
    activate BFF
    BFF->>MDM: GET /api/meter/{id}
    activate MDM

    alt Meter Available
        MDM->>DB: Query Meter Details
        activate DB
        DB-->>MDM: Water Address, Property Type, Past Reads
        deactivate DB
        MDM-->>BFF: 200 OK (Meter Details)
        deactivate MDM
        BFF-->>Mobile: Meter Details
        deactivate BFF
        Mobile-->>User: Display Details

        User->>Mobile: Validate Address

        alt Address is Not Valid
            alt Address Available in Map
                User->>Mobile: Select Address from Map
                Mobile->>BFF: POST /address/select
                activate BFF
                BFF-->>Mobile: Address Confirmed
                deactivate BFF
            else Address Not in Map
                User->>Mobile: Collect Data for Unique Address ID
                Mobile->>BFF: POST /address/create
                activate BFF
                BFF->>MDM: Create Address
                activate MDM
                MDM->>DB: Insert New Address
                activate DB
                DB-->>MDM: Success
                deactivate DB
                MDM-->>BFF: Address Created
                deactivate MDM
                BFF-->>Mobile: Success
                deactivate BFF
            end
        end

        User->>Mobile: Collect Meter Read
        User->>Mobile: Submit Form
        Mobile->>BFF: POST /meter-read
        activate BFF
        BFF->>MDM: POST /api/readings
        activate MDM
        MDM->>DB: Insert Reading
        activate DB
        DB-->>MDM: Success
        deactivate DB
        MDM-->>BFF: 201 Created
        deactivate MDM
        BFF-->>Mobile: 201 Created
        deactivate BFF
        Mobile-->>User: Submission Success

    else Meter Not Available
        MDM-->>BFF: 404 Not Found (No Meter)
        deactivate MDM
        BFF-->>Mobile: Error (No Meter)
        deactivate BFF
        Mobile-->>User: Display Error
    end
```

input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

input_prompt:

sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

else BFF should respond with user profile

nl_task_title: User registration and account creation process 6/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: User registration and account creation process
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Database, Email Service
Interactions:
1. User Registration: The user fills out the registration form on the mobile app.
2. Data Submission: The mobile app sends the registration data to the BFF.
3. Data Validation: The BFF validates the registration data (e.g., checks for existing users, valid email format).
4. Database Insertion: The BFF inserts the validated data into the database, creating a new user account.
5. Email Verification: The BFF sends a verification email to the user's email address using the Email Service.
6. Email Confirmation: The user clicks the verification link in the email, which redirects to the mobile app.
7. Account Activation: The mobile app sends a confirmation to the BFF, which activates the user's account in the database.
8. Success Notification: The mobile app displays a success message to the user, indicating that the account has been created and verified.

input_prompt:

sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```

sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Mobile as Mobile App
    participant BFF
    participant DB as Database
    participant Email as Email Service

    User->>Mobile: Submit Registration Data
    activate Mobile

    end

nl_task_title: Online payment processing for an e-commerce application 7/I
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Online payment processing for an e-commerce application
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Payment Gateway, Database
Interactions:
1. Product Selection: The user selects products and proceeds to checkout on the mobile app.
2. Payment Request: The mobile app sends the payment details (e.g., amount, payment method) to the BFF.
3. Payment Gateway Interaction: The BFF forwards the payment details to the Payment Gateway for processing.
4. Payment Authorization: The Payment Gateway authorizes the payment and returns a transaction ID to the BFF.
5. Transaction Confirmation: The BFF updates the transaction status in the database and sends a confirmation to the mobile app.
6. Order Confirmation: The mobile app displays an order confirmation message to the user.
7. Error Handling: If the payment fails, the Payment Gateway returns an error code, and the BFF notifies the mobile app to display an error message.

nl_task_title: Online payment processing for an e-commerce application 7/II
nl_task_desc:
Purpose: Online payment processing for an e-commerce application
Main Components: User, Mobile App, BFF, Payment Gateway, Database
Interactions:
1. Product Selection: The user selects products and proceeds to checkout on the mobile app.
2. Payment Request: The mobile app sends the payment details (e.g., amount, payment method) to the BFF.
3. Payment Gateway Interaction: The BFF forwards the payment details to the Payment Gateway for processing.
4. Payment Authorization: The Payment Gateway authorizes the payment and returns a transaction ID to the BFF.
5. Transaction Confirmation: The BFF updates the transaction status in the database and sends a confirmation to the mobile app.
6. Order Confirmation: The mobile app displays an order confirmation message to the user.
7. Error Handling: If the payment fails, the Payment Gateway returns an error code, and the BFF notifies the mobile app to display an error message.
input_prompt:
Generate a valid, syntactically correct MermaidJS UML
sequence diagram based on the provided structured input:

Input Format:
- Purpose: Description of the diagram
- Main Components: List of involved components
- Interactions: Sequence of interactions

General Guidelines:
- Identify interactions between the Main Components, distinguishing requests (->>) and responses (-->>). 
- Structure participants in this order: Users -> Frontend (Mobile/Web) -> Backend (BFF, Middleware, SOR, DB).
- Determine all the alternate cases needed, if-else, alt, and make sure they are all covered.
- Make sure there is a response to every request.
- Format API calls as REST API (e.g., `GET /Workorder(params)`, `POST /Workorder(params)`).
- Handle alternate flows (if, if-else, nested conditions) per MermaidJS syntax.
- Ensure syntax correctness, covering all responses, error scenarios, and system failures.
- Use activation (`activate` actor) and deactivation (`deactivate` actor) properly.
- Add notes where applicable.

MermaidJS Syntax Rules:
- Begin with 'sequenceDiagram'.
- Use 'participant ActorName' to introduce actors.
- Activate ('activate') actors when they receive messages; deactivate ('deactivate') after responding.
- Treat alternate case like "if{}", "if{} else {}", nested if statements has to be handled.
- Ensure that any actor activated before an alternate flow must deactivate after it ends.
- Follow structured handling of conditional cases (see Example).
- Add notes over the actor only when required. Notes should address the question of "why". Notes can also be used to segregate groups of request/response flows. Example: `Note over Actor: notes content`
- Respond only with the MermaidJS code, formatted as markdown. Do not include explanations or additional text.

Example Input:
Purpose: Client-Server Request Handling
Main Components: Client (CT), Server (SR)
Interactions:
Client Action: The client sends a request with data (X) to the server.
Server Processing:
    - The server receives the request and processes it.
    - If the request is valid, the server sends a successful response with data (Y).
    - If the request is invalid, the server returns an error message indicating invalid data.
Response:
    - Upon successful processing, the client receives the requested data.
    - If an error occurs, the client is notified accordingly.

Example Output:
```sequenceDiagram
participant CT as Client
participant SR as Server
Note over CT: Sending a request
CT->>SR: Request (Data: X)
activate SR
alt Successful Response
    SR-->>CT: Response (Data: Y)
else Error Case
    SR-->>CT: Error (Invalid Data)
end
deactivate SR```
